data.title: Oracle: Sell Before The AI Bubble Bursts
data.description:
The depreciation of Oracle Corporation's (ORCL) shares has been significant in recent months, but it is unlikely to be over. While the company was able to greatly increase its remaining performance obligations in the last two quarters, there's a risk that the business itself will get limited returns from the AI-related deals that it signed in 2025. At the same time, as the overall market sentiment slowly changes and the risk premium for AI-related stocks rises, there's a risk that Oracle's stock could lose even more value and disappoint investors in 2026.

The Sentiment Is Shifting

Oracle made headlines in September after it signed a 5-year, $300 billion deal with OpenAI under which it will supply the computing power to the creator of ChatGPT. As a result of this, Oracle's remaining performance obligations surged, its stock jumped to new highs, and the company's founder, Larry Ellison, at one point became the world's richest person. However, despite all the optimism surrounding the deal, Oracle's stock nevertheless began to lose traction shortly after reaching new highs and has lost all the value that it previously gained since the deal with OpenAI was signed.

One of the biggest catalysts behind the latest depreciation is the shift in sentiment and the change in perception of AI-related businesses by the market. The MIT study from last year showed that 95% of organizations currently get zero returns on their AI investments, while one of HSBC's latest reports indicates that OpenAI itself is unlikely to become profitable by the end of this decade. As a result, some investors have become skeptical about the ability of OpenAI to commit around $1 trillion to deals that it signed with companies like Oracle and others, especially since it's expected to report around a $10 billion loss for fiscal 2025.

That's why it's not a surprise that investors in recent months started to look for hedging instruments in case the overall AI-related spending comes to a halt if some big companies or hyperscalers are unable to fulfill their parts of the deal. The fact that the spread for Oracle's credit default swaps in recent months reached the highest level since the Great Recession shows that the risk of an AI bubble that could pop at any moment is real. Therefore, even Oracle's successful Q2 earnings report, which was released last month and showed that the revenues and the remaining performance obligations increased, didn't stop the depreciation of the company's shares.

More importantly, to fulfill its part of the deal, Oracle would need to commit significant resources to buy the land and build the data centers, which will be able to provide computing power to its customers. To get those resources, Oracle would likely be prompted to reach out to the debt market if it wants to build the expensive data centers. The reason why we're thinking that debt financing is one of the few options that Oracle has is that the company started to significantly burn cash in recent months. During the Q1 earnings call in September, the company noted that its free cash flow in the last four quarters was negative $5.9 billion. Then, during the Q2 earnings call in December, the management stated that in the latest quarter alone, the free cash flow was a negative $10 billion. This shows that its business can no longer cover the existing expenditures that remain significant and continue to rise. For a company that has a debt-to-equity ratio of over 500% and a net debt position of $111.96 billion at the end of last quarter, this is a problem. If FCF remains negative in the foreseeable future, which is likely given the rising CapEx, Oracle might reach a point when it will become much harder for it to service all that debt.

Also, considering that the company's own report from October showed that the business of renting computing power to others is challenging and has thin margins, there's a risk that after building all the data centers, the return on their investments will be limited. What's worse is that even if the margins issue is resolved at the beginning, a significant amount of resources that are allocated to building data centers are spent on buying powerful GPUs. Considering that the lifespan of the latest GPUs is mostly up to six years, while more powerful chips are expected to be released quite frequently, there's a risk that Oracle will be left with obsolete AI accelerators fairly quickly and won't be able to generate decent returns.

That's why it's not a surprise that the market's optimism has quickly faded, and Oracle's shares have been mostly depreciating since late September. The fact that the company became a target of a class action lawsuit last week due to the AI-related losses just shows that investors are losing confidence in Oracle's ability to generate any decent returns from its business of providing computing power to its customers.

The Real Value of Oracle

With the amount of challenges that Oracle is facing, we believe that its shares still have room to fall, and we created a valuation model to figure out the company's intrinsic value. In the model, we used a 3% perpetual growth rate, which is the same rate as we use most of the time when figuring out the value of other companies. The rate is also similar to the historical GDP and inflation rate. Considering that the perpetual rate basically refers to the growth rate at which the company will grow indefinitely, giving a greater rate makes no sense since under such a scenario, at one point, the business will be worth more than the overall economy, which is not a realistic possibility.

The data for the cash reserves and debt were taken from the recent earnings report.

The discount rate in the model is 8.58%, which we figured out after calculating Oracle's cost of debt and cost of equity. The data for calculating the cost of debt was mostly taken from the trailing twelve months. For the cost of equity, we used a risk-free rate of 4.15%, a beta of 1.57, and a market return rate of 7.84%.

In the forecast table below, our sales assumptions are similar to the overall consensus, while the EBIT margin is expected to gradually improve over time. The tax rate in the model is the same as in the previous year.

As for the CapEx, during the Q1 earnings call, Oracle's management expected the expenditures to reach $35 billion. However, during the Q2 earnings call, the management said that they now expect the expenditures to be $15 billion over the Q1 assumptions. The expenditures are expected to be used on expanding the business to honor its remaining performance in the following years. Considering this, we reflected an increase in CapEx in FY26 and beyond. At the same time, we also increased the assumptions for the D&A due to fairly quick depreciation of GPUs, which are going to account for a significant portion of CapEx. The change in net working capital as a percentage of sales is similar to Oracle's historical performance.

Our model shows that Oracle's enterprise value is $500.99 billion, while its equity value is $389.03 billion. That translates to an intrinsic value of $133.14 per share, which is below the current market price by around 33%.

Risks To Our Bearish Thesis

Despite all the challenges, Oracle nevertheless reaffirmed its guidance last month and could even raise the manageable level of debt to fund its projects, which could make it possible for the company to undermine our bearish thesis.

At the same time, as hyperscalers are expected to spend record sums on scaling their AI capabilities in 2026, while the overall economy is also forecasted to grow at a decent rate this year, there's a possibility that the stock market will continue to grow. This could help Oracle's shares find a solid technical support level and potentially rebound over time.

Final Thoughts

The massive depreciation of Oracle's stock in recent months indicates that the market is questioning the latest deals that the business signed with major AI companies and is not convinced that significant returns from those deals are fully guaranteed. As the overall sentiment surrounding AI stocks is shifting and the risk premium rises, investors might benefit more from buying safer assets in the current environment. Considering that Oracle is also overvalued even after the latest depreciation, we find it hard to see a major rebound of its shares anytime soon. For that reason, we decided to rate Oracle as a Sell.

data.title: Alger Spectra Fund Q4 2025 Portfolio Update
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Alger Spectra Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

Class A shares of the Alger Spectra Fund underperformed the Russell 3000 Growth Index during the fourth quarter of 2025. Alphabet Inc. (GOOG), Natera, Inc. (NTRA), and Cidara Therapeutics, Inc. (CDTX) were among the top contributors to performance.

Alphabet is the parent of Google and a global leader in digital advertising, cloud computing, and AI-driven consumer services. Shares contributed positively during the quarter as investors increasingly recognized improving execution across the company’s AI strategy, supported by strong engagement trends and rising AI-related workloads. Google Cloud was a key driver, with management highlighting accelerating usage, including increased AI token processing and continued momentum in the Gemini ecosystem. Sentiment was further supported by strengthening cloud demand and new customer wins, as well as reports that Google may broaden access to its TPU capabilities, including potential deployments beyond Google Cloud.

Natera is a specialty diagnostics laboratory that provides high-value genetic testing across three core franchises: reproductive health (including non-invasive prenatal testing for chromosomal conditions such as trisomy 13, 18, and 21), oncology (led by Signatera, which measures circulating tumor DNA to assess treatment response and detect molecular residual disease and cancer recurrence), and transplant (tests used to monitor organ rejection). These tests are built on the company’s proprietary cell-free DNA liquid biopsy platform, enabling highly sensitive detection from a blood sample. Shares contributed positively after the company reported strong fiscal third-quarter earnings, with management commentary pointing to revenue and margin performance that exceeded expectations on the back of higher test volumes and favorable reimbursement dynamics— particularly within the Signatera franchise—alongside another increase to full-year 2025 revenue and margin guidance.

Cidara Therapeutics is a biotechnology company developing drug-Fc conjugate (DFC) therapeutics, with its lead program CD388, an investigational long-acting, strain-agnostic antiviral designed to prevent influenza infection in individuals at higher risk of complications. CD388 was supported by compelling Phase 2b NAVIGATE results and advanced into the Phase 3 ANCHOR study, positioning it as a potentially first-in-class, single-dose prophylactic option for influenza prevention. Share contributed positively after Merck announced an all-cash agreement to acquire Cidara for approximately $9.2 billion.

Our short exposure to a global technology company contributed positively to performance. During the quarter, management issued weaker-than-expected forward earnings guidance and announced a restructuring plan that included meaningful headcount reductions, reinforcing concerns about near-term profitability. Investors also remained skeptical that strength in PCs could offset continued softness in printing. As a result, the company’s share price declined during the quarter, benefiting our short position.

Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), and Meta Platforms Inc. (META) were among the top detractors from performance.

Nebius is a provider of AI-focused cloud infrastructure, operating GPU compute capacity across Europe and expanding in the U.S. We view the company as a differentiated “neocloud” beneficiary of accelerating AI adoption, as demand for high-quality GPU compute continues to outstrip available supply of chips, power, and data center capacity. Nebius already operates a first-party data center in Finland and has deployed GPU capacity in Paris, while adding U.S. capacity through a Kansas City, Missouri deployment and a new data center build-out in New Jersey. During the quarter, shares detracted amid a broader selloff in AI infrastructure-related equities as investors grew more concerned about the “circularity” of AI ecosystem financing and the durability of AI infrastructure spending. Despite near-term volatility, Nebius continues to sign large, long-term agreements with tier-one customers, including multi-year AI infrastructure contracts with major cloud service providers, improving revenue visibility as new capacity comes online.

Microsoft is a beneficiary of corporate America’s transformative digitization. The company operates through three segments: Productivity and Business Processes (Office365, LinkedIn, and Dynamics), Intelligent Cloud (Server Products and Cloud Services, Azure, and Enterprise Services), and More Personal Computing (Windows, Devices, Gaming, and Search). Shares detracted during the quarter after the company reported Azure cloud growth and forward guidance fell short of elevated investor expectations, in part because revenue recognition lagged strong demand and the company remained capacity constrained. While Azure still grew 39% year-over-year (YoY) and management guided to 37% growth in the current quarter, we believe investors focused on the near-term mismatch between demand and available capacity. Importantly, demand signals improved: commercial bookings surged (reported up approximately 111% YoY) and remaining performance obligations rose 51% compared to 37% from the prior quarter, extending revenue visibility. To address the backlog and support AI/cloud workloads, management signaled a step-up in investment, including roughly $30B of capital expenditures (CapEx) in the current quarter and an expectation that fiscal-2026 CapEx growth will be higher than fiscal-2025.

Meta is the world’s largest social-media company, operating platforms that include Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, and Messenger. Shares detracted during the quarter as investors focused on management’s guidance for materially higher operating expenses and capital expenditures (CapEx) tied to AI infrastructure investments. Management noted that 2026 CapEx is expected to be “notably” higher than 2025, which pressured forward earnings and free-cash-flow expectations despite strong underlying results. While the company highlighted continued advertising momentum and indicated that revenue growth could remain robust into year-end, investors remained concerned about the magnitude and timing of the AI-related CapEx.

Our short exposure to a system integrator of utility-scale battery storage detracted from performance. During the quarter, the company reported mixed fiscal fourth-quarter results, with revenue missing expectations due to ongoing domestic manufacturing ramp- up issues. Despite the revenue miss, the company posted stronger- than-expected margins and delivered a record quarter for orders and backlog, prompting management to initiate fiscal 2026 revenue guidance that investors viewed as more feasible given substantial backlog support. Management also pointed to an emerging data center opportunity, citing a largely new behind-the-meter pipeline and an expanding total addressable market that could contribute more meaningfully over the longer term. The stock rose on these developments, pressuring our short position during the quarter.

The first full calendar year that Fred Alger Management, LLC was the Fund’s investment manager was 1975; therefore, since inception performance is reflected as of this date. The Fund operated as a closed end fund from August 23, 1978 to February 12, 1996. The calculation of total return during that time assumes dividends were reinvested at market value. Had dividends not been reinvested, performance would have been lower. On September 24, 2008, the Fund’s name was changed from Spectra Fund to Alger Spectra Fund, and the Fund’s Class N shares were redesignated as Class A shares. Performance from February 13, 1996 through September 23, 2008 is that of the Fund’s Class N Shares, adjusted to reflect applicable sales charges and operating expenses.

data.title: Alger Spectra Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Investment Strategy

Primarily invests in growth equity securities of U.S. companies identified through our fundamental research as demonstrating promising growth potential, and engages in short selling (up to approximately 10% of the market value of the portfolio). Seeks long-term capital appreciation.

Highlights

During the fourth quarter of 2025, the largest portfolio sector weightings were Information Technology and Communication Services. The largest sector overweight was Communication Services and the largest sector underweight was Health Care.

The Information Technology and Utilities sectors contributed to relative performance while Health Care and Communication Services were among sectors that detracted from relative performance.

Market Environment

U.S. equities ended the fourth quarter on a firm footing, with the S&P 500 Index returning 2.7%, extending the steady grind upward that characterized 2025, after the tariff announcements. Investor confidence was supported by a familiar trio: corporate earnings that came in better than expected, a Federal Reserve (Fed) that pushed further into an easing stance, and a macroeconomic backdrop that — despite mounting cross-currents — remained resilient in the aggregate. Market sentiment also benefited from improving clarity on trade policy, including an extension of the U.S. – China truce and selective tariff relief aimed at easing consumer affordability pressures.

At the same time, the quarter was defined by widening dispersion beneath the index-level surface. The market’s biggest tailwind — continued investment tied to artificial intelligence (AI) — was increasingly accompanied by scrutiny around AI infrastructure bottlenecks, sources of financing, and whether the AI theme could deliver returns commensurate with the capital deployed. Macroeconomic risks also remained in play: labor market softening became harder to ignore, consumers appeared more cautious and bifurcated across income cohorts, and housing stayed under pressure. Complicating the read-through, a prolonged government shutdown reduced economic data visibility, amplifying debate over inflation and employment signals just as global central-bank dynamics leaned less uniformly supportive. Nevertheless, U.S. equities reinforced a constructive baseline for the S&P 500 Index, where Health Care was the top performing sector, while Real Estate was the weakest.

During the quarter, we continued to observe secular themes that we believe are creating attractive investment opportunities—corporations are digitizing their operations, cloud computing is growing and supporting innovation, and AI is at an inflection point, potentially enabling significant increases in productivity. Moreover, U.S. business spending appears to be accelerating, driven by a confluence of factors—most notably burgeoning demand for AI infrastructure and tax incentives from the One Big Beautiful Bill, which allow companies to deduct 100% of eligible equipment and qualifying manufacturing-facility costs in the year placed in service, boosting near-term cash flow and after-tax returns.

data.title: AST SpaceMobile: The Story Is Exploding, But Don't Chase The Breakout Yet
data.description:
I thought the bubble on AST SpaceMobile, Inc. (ASTS) would implode to end 2025, but I was wrong. Not only did ASTS defy my wildest imagination (and expectations), but the stock of the space-based cellular broadband network operator surged well >130% since my last ASTS writeup in June 2025. I must say I didn't fathom such a breathtaking riposte to what I thought was a reasonably conceived caution (I indicated a Hold/Neutral rating in case you think I was bearish). More importantly, I didn't acknowledge a Sell position was necessary, given the ascendency of the industry (leveraging off Elon Musk's SpaceX-Starlink's (SPACE) preeminence) and the potential multi-trillion dollar IPO this year that could also see Musk attain the unbelievable trillionaire status even before he achieves the milestones embedded in his recently revised Tesla (TSLA) compensation.

For AST SpaceMobile, the near-term catalysts are almost about making sure that their launch cadence is carefully maintained and executed with little fuss. These are the foundational pillars of its nascent business model that must be executed almost flawlessly in laying the path to achieving 45-60 satellites in orbit by the end of 2026. Competition to help ASTS launch these satellites into space has also heated up recently, and the company indicates that it's taking a long-term view with their contracted providers, while also making sure that they have signed up for additional launches, which could scale to >100 satellites over time.

The market's reaction to these potential catalysts is clearly fast and furious. Incredibly, while there are obviously pretty steep pullbacks along the way, dip buyers have consistently returned to help the stock break into new highs subsequently. That pattern has persisted with unwavering conviction even through this week's highs, as the company announced that it has been a “prime contract” for the “US Missile Defense Agency SHIELD program.”

There were already prior expectations that ASTS could participate in one way or another, as the potential of “dual use tech” is now coming into play, as part of the US's proposed Golden Dome missile defense system. While this doesn't immediately confer a substantive revenue pathway or pipeline (yet) skewing in favor of ASTS's pipeline in the near term, I'm in concurrence with management's assertion that the contract “validates its unique, on-orbit, dual-use technology and our growing capabilities within the defense sector.”

As a note, ASTS had already remarked in December on how AST SpaceMobile is primed to partake, given the array of strategic imperatives that the US government intends to deploy, leveraging on the firm and its leading peers in their core capabilities, while also avoiding the so-called “vendor lock” (presumably or particularly with SpaceX) that the US government is arguably keen to abstain from given the extremely high stakes involved in this massive initiative:

We can do other things with our frequencies, including RADAR, that support a lot of very strategic capabilities for the U.S. government and its allies. So that is what's brought us into the Golden Dome conversation as it's evolved over the past year, where we see -- we had a very positive U.S. government investment space backdrop over the last 5 to 10 years. But with this year, it's really accelerated. There's currently a $150 billion RFP open for a lot of the stuff that's involved and will be involved with Golden Dome over time. These are really big use cases that are really relevant where the government is looking for dual-use capabilities, which means government services, free riding or riding on commercial investment, which is what we are, as well as avoiding vendor lock, which means you're not stuck in a contract with 1 prime for a decade because you've made some investment. You want to dual source it. - AST SpaceMobile presents at UBS Global Media and Communications Conference 2025

Such developments, I believe, should serve to layer on top of the burgeoning source of commercial partnerships that have been struck with an increasingly larger slate of MNO partners. These were highlighted earlier in ASTS's Q3 earnings scorecard. I believe the core notion that is underlining the company's narrative right now isn't so much about whether the tech still needs further validation, but rather whether they can effectively monetize these partnerships quickly as they get those launches into orbit.

Therefore, while the $1B in contracted revenue serves to assure investors of the potential for revenue conversion, execution will still hinge upon the launches and deliveries, as the market rechannels its focus from liquidity (reportedly having $3.2B in pro forma cash and equivalents) and technological capabilities. The milestones enumerated in the Block 2 Bluebird deployment plan will help investors ascertain whether the target could lean closer to the low end of the schedule, or could it skew toward the 60 satellites mark by the end of this year.

Hence, I think it's pivotal for investors to scrutinize the earlier developments in the key markets identified for early 2026, namely in the US, Canada, Japan, Saudi Arabia, and the UK. For astute ASTS investors, I believe they already have an inkling that while the near-term margins could come under pressure, the deployment sequences have afforded the market a semblance of clarity in the near term.

Hence, the focus has decidedly shifted to the potential for long-term earnings accretion, as compared to worrying incessantly about short-term liquidity constraints, which doesn't look like they could hamper ASTS significantly for now, given the substantive liquidity raise it undertook in 2025, which bolstered its cash runway markedly. And the level of operating leverage will very much depend on how quickly management scales its launches and converts the contracted revenue, which should percolate into its bottomline, augmenting its self-sufficiency and reducing the uncertain cash flow variability in its forward outlook:

So when you look through the history of satellite companies the last 20 years, when the businesses have been doing well and growing, you see 85% margins for wholesale businesses. And when you dig into those companies, even today, where there's attractive segments, there can be 90% plus marginal economics, EBITDA margin flow-through margin. So for us, there's a big CapEx upfront. We all know that. That's why we funded the business the way we have. Once it's in orbit, very little cost to maintain. There'll be a small maintenance CapEx line item over time as the 7- to 10-year cycle of the asset is recycled. But our OpEx, which is in the $60 million per quarter today, might trend up a little bit with growth. The opportunities we're seeing are phenomenal. But if you're looking at that plus some lease expense, you still have a pretty fixed margin business. We have some revenue shares, including with the operators, but those will all come out of the -- before we get the gross revenue on our income statement. So the flow-through margin here and the operating leverage is pretty special, and we expect that to be in excess of 90% over time. - AST SpaceMobile presents at UBS Global Media and Communications Conference 2025

So, this is precisely what we see right now, isn't it? Wall Street is clearly baking in a more robust ramp and revenue accretion through 2028 and beyond. Hence, the near-term focus will be to get those satellites into orbit throughout 2026, and then we should see the monetization economies starting to cascade into ASTS's P&L with some patience.

Well, that's (however) obviously easier said than done, as the focus turns undoubtedly to deployment and execution. But I think what the market has also attempted to bake into ASTS's valuation multiples (almost 190x on a forward revenue basis based on enterprise value) is that we should be looking at the stock as an end-of-the-decade free cash flow story.

The current liquidity buffer indicates that ASTS should be well-positioned to maintain its cadence. The participation in the SHIED program corroborates a superior growth optionality that should enhance the thesis of ASTS.

Nevertheless, while I think the stock is seemingly leaning even more bullish with the higher-lows and higher-highs market print reflected on its price action, I'm still somewhat cautious as I want to see how the 2026 deployment schedules proceed first.

And we also know that the stock is inherently volatile, so I'm quite sure that there are opportunities for investors who are thinking about loading up at the next pullback after the current euphoria settles down a little. If I think it's truly worth a shot, I will most certainly inform subscribers first in my Investing Group: Ultimate Growth Investing so that they can also consider taking part in this new growth vector for their portfolio.

Rating: Maintain Hold.

Purchase durable business franchises that are selling at a discount to their intrinsic value.

We utilize a business owner's approach to investing, thoroughly investigating the economics of the business and the quality of the management team. Some of the characteristics of good businesses include strong recurring revenue and attractive returns-on-invested capital (ROIC). We have a strong orientation to low absolute and relative valuation, which are key to the execution of our investment strategy. A new idea will come from a variety of sources including company visits, screens, conferences, trade periodicals and general reading. All members of the research team are responsible for fundamental research.

Once an investment opportunity is identified it is put through an extensive due diligence process, which typically includes management interviews and site visits. When an acceptable level of conviction is achieved, the appropriate weighting (considering liquidity, valuation, etc.) is discussed and determined. A new company purchased in the portfolio will usually have an initial position size of 1-3%. The portfolio generally consists of 30-40 companies and is diversified across industries.

We are long-term investors, a typical holding period for our companies is three to five years, and portfolio turnover averages 20-40% per annum.

data.title: Boralex: Underfollowed Renewable Energy Company Trading At A Discount
data.description:
In the short run, the market is a voting machine but in the long run, it is a weighing machine. - Benjamin Graham

Boralex (BRLXF)(BLX:CA) is the kind of company that does not generate much enthusiasm. It keeps building renewable generation assets at a steady pace, but compared to some AI and other technology companies, it can feel like watching paint dry. Despite the lack of excitement, the company has been a solid value compounder, growing free cash flow at a faster pace than most of its peers. It also pays a decent dividend and has a number of structural tailwinds that could accelerate growth in the coming years.

That boredom is part of the appeal. Boralex has largely contracted cash flows, a healthy balance sheet, and optionality in the form of a large development pipeline. Rising electricity demand from data centers, industrial automation, and electric vehicles (EVs) makes the pipeline more valuable, especially when considering the rapidly increasing average price of electricity in many countries where it operates. For example, in the U.S., retail electricity prices have outpaced CPI inflation over the past five years. The company has exposure to four main markets: Canada, the U.S., France, and the U.K. If Graham is right, even though Boralex is not exactly popular with investors right now, the valuation should eventually reflect the real value of the business.

Company Overview

Boralex is both a developer and an operator of renewable energy assets, particularly onshore solar and wind. It ended the third quarter with a total installed power capacity of 3.4 GW, and with an additional 558 MW in projects under construction or ready to be built. It has two core markets, France and Canada, and two expansion markets, the U.S. and the U.K. Historically, it has underperformed the S&P 500 Index (SP500)(SPY), in particular the last few years. It has, however, significantly outperformed the iShares Global Clean Energy ETF (ICLN).

Boralex has a reputation as a disciplined and experienced developer of renewable energy assets. We have followed the company for some time, but there were a couple of things that kept us away from considering the shares. One was an elevated valuation; the second was its excessive focus on wind energy. Onshore wind and solar projects are relatively easy to replicate compared to hydro or energy storage.

Things are changing, though, as more than half of the 8 GW pipeline is made up of solar and energy storage projects. This should add some diversification, and the energy storage projects in particular offer more differentiation opportunities.

Boralex is proving that it can deliver its energy storage projects without major issues, with Tilbury, its first North American project, recently commissioned. The much larger Hagersville is scheduled for the coming weeks. Together they should result in 380 MW (1,520 MWh) of energy storage capacity for Boralex in Canada.

Balance Sheet

Boralex also has the advantage of a solid balance sheet, with most debt being long-term project debt. Only about 13% of its debt is at the corporate level. Boralex ended the third quarter with CAD $811 million in available liquidity it can use to advance its in-progress projects and seize attractive M&A opportunities if they present themselves.

Shareholder Returns

Boralex's dividend yield is below that of most of its peers, but it is a choice made by the company in order to reduce its reliance on equity issuance to help finance new projects. At close to 2.5%, it is not insignificant, and it is slightly above its ten-year average.

The payout ratio is relatively low, which reduces the risk of a potential dividend cut. This applies to both the payout ratio based on adjusted Funds from Operations (AFFO) and on Boralex's net cash flows from operating activities.

Future Outlook

Boralex has a target to reach roughly 7 GW of capacity by 2030, which would be more than twice where they finished the third quarter of 2025. Boralex is also targeting longer-duration contracts, with the objective of reaching a weighted average of close to 14 years, up from the current ~12 years.

Where it gets interesting is looking at how the increase in generating capacity translates into higher free cash flow (FCF) per share. Management has shared a measure, which they call "discretionary cash flow per share", which stood at CAD $1.54 in 2024 and has been guided to grow at a compounded annual growth rate (CAGR) of between 8% to 10% through 2030. Boralex is currently trading close to CAD $26, or a roughly 6% free cash flow yield on 2024's results, and a ~10% free cash flow yield based on 2030 guidance.

Valuation

Boralex appears undervalued when compared to its historical valuation multiples. From EV/EBITDA to Price/Cash Flow from Operations to Price/Book, they are all below their ten-year averages. In particular, the Price/Book value ratio has significantly compressed, currently at close to 33% of the ten-year average.

Risks

The main risk we see with Boralex lies in the development of new projects, where going over budget or experiencing delays can be expensive. It is therefore reassuring to see that it is completing energy storage projects without major issues, as this is a relatively new technology for the company.

The existing asset base is mostly contracted, but there is a small percentage that operates on a merchant basis, where there is price risk. Lower short-term electricity prices in France were a key reason why the company reported a slightly lower EBITDA in the third quarter. Another important risk comes from weather variations, in particular average wind speed and solar radiation.

Takeaway

Boralex has become a much more interesting stock to consider at the current valuation. Especially with rapidly growing demand for electricity, expansion into energy storage, and a path to double installed capacity by 2030. The company has a long growth runway and is compounding value by retaining more than half of its free cash flow. Even with a modest payout ratio, the dividend yield is not insignificant at close to 2.5%. If management can deliver on their 2030 guidance of 8% to 10% "discretionary cash flow" compounded annual growth, there is certainly potential for 10%+ total returns for investors.

data.title: PepsiCo: A Blue-Chip Dividend Aristocrat And Dividend King Ripe For Picking
data.description:
PepsiCo Inc. (PEP) is an American multinational food, snack, and beverage corporation. Founded in 1898, PepsiCo is now a $191 billion (by market cap) snack and beverage giant that employs nearly 320,000 people.

The company reports results across seven segments: PepsiCo Beverages North America, 30% of FY 2024 revenue; Frito-Lay North America, 27%; Europe, 15%; Latin America, 13%; Africa, Middle East, and South Asia, 7%; APAC (Asia Pacific), 5%; and Quaker Foods North America, 3%. The company generates approximately 56% of revenue from the US; the remainder is generated internationally.

PepsiCo runs a simple but very effective business model: It sells cherished beverages and snack foods to billions of people across the world. This simplicity and effectiveness has allowed PepsiCo to thrive for more than 125 years and build itself a beverage and snack empire.

While taste is subjective, there’s clearly some common ground among billions of people. I say that because PepsiCo has 23 different billion-dollar brands (i.e., brands that do more than $1 billion per year in sales), including the likes of Aquafina, Doritos, Lay’s, Mountain Dew, and the eponymous Pepsi.

By providing consistent and desirable taste profiles across these branded products, PepsiCo has built up a loyal global customer base seeking the familiar – which allows the company to leverage pricing power and reinforce its resilience. And since beverages and snacks must be repurchased once consumed, the company has an incredible amount of recurring and enduring revenue, which has translated into decades of profit and dividend growth.

Dividend Growth, Growth Rate, Payout Ratio and Yield

Indeed, PepsiCo has increased its dividend for a whopping 53 consecutive years. That qualifies it for its status as a vaunted Dividend Aristocrat more than twice over.

It’s a Dividend King – and a quintessential one, at that. Its 10-year dividend growth rate is 7.4%, which is very solid for a large, mature company multiple decades into its dividend growth story, although more recent dividend raises have trended closer to the mid-single-digit area.

And you get to layer that dividend growth on top of the stock’s yield of 3.9%. To see a stock of this stature offer a yield this high is honestly kind of shocking. We’re in REIT and utility territory here. To put things in perspective, this yield is 100 basis points higher than its own five-year average.

The one issue here with the dividend is the high payout ratio. Based on TTM Core EPS, that number is 72.5%. Making matters worse, the dividend is sucking up pretty much all of the company’s free cash flow.

While I don’t see the dividend as being in immediate danger, and while I’d really like to see a larger margin of safety here, the bigger issue for now is that dividend growth will be limited to, at best, whatever the business itself can generate (although dividend growth should actually be lower than business growth in order to get the payout ratio down).

Despite that quick warning, this remains a Dividend King with the utmost commitment to its outsized dividend. For income-oriented investors, it’s awfully appealing to be able to capture a near-4% yield from a world-class consumer products company.

Revenue and Earnings Growth

As appealing as it may be, though, that largely draws on past data. However, investors must always be thinking about the future, as the capital of today gets risked for the rewards of tomorrow. Thus, I’ll now build out a forward-looking growth trajectory for the business, which will be useful for the valuation process.

I’ll first show you what the business has done over the last decade in terms of its top-line and bottom-line growth. And I’ll then reveal a professional prognostication for near-term profit growth. Lining up the proven past with a future forecast in this manner should give us a base of information upon which we can start to draw conclusions about where the business could be going from here.

PepsiCo moved its revenue from $63.1 billion in FY 2015 to $91.9 billion in FY 2024. That’s a compound annual growth rate of 4.3%. For a mature company that was already more than 100 years old and starting off with a sales base north of $60 billion, compounding the top line at a 4%+ clip annually is actually quite solid.

Meanwhile, earnings per share increased from $3.67 to $6.95 over this period, which is a CAGR of 7.4%. We can see that dividend growth and EPS growth over the last decade have mirrored each other precisely, showing deft control from management.

Consistent buybacks helped to drive excess bottom-line growth, with the outstanding share count down by more than 7% over the last 10 years. Again, for such a large, mature company with what could be described as saturated markets, this is very respectable growth.

Looking forward, CFRA is projecting a 3% CAGR for PepsiCo’s EPS over the next three years. This would represent a more than 50% deceleration in bottom-line growth, relative to what PepsiCo generated over the prior decade. While CFRA lauds the company’s quality and earnings resiliency, as well as the power of its various brands, it’s hard to ignore recent organic volume trends.

PepsiCo’s growth of late has been driven by price taking, which stemmed from inflation hitting the company’s input costs hard during the pandemic, but this taking of price hurt volumes. Consumers are simply scaling back on purchases as costs rise.

In addition, there’s the ongoing question of what GLP-1s will ultimately do demand for the types of beverages and snacks that PepsiCo offers, although the company has been shifting its portfolio toward healthier options through both in-house moves and acquisitions. The company’s 2025 acquisition of fast-growing prebiotic soda brand Poppi for just under $1.7 billion is a perfect example of this.

I think CFRA’s caution is warranted, and it’s fair to assume modest growth out of PepsiCo over the next few years. However, as prices settle, margins stabilize, and the healthier portfolio starts to shine, something closer to the company’s historical growth rate could certainly return.

Meanwhile, those getting in now are locking in that near-4% yield while awaiting the tide to turn.

Financial Position

Moving over to the balance sheet, PepsiCo has a good financial position.

The long-term debt/equity ratio is 2, while the interest coverage ratio is 14. Because of low common equity, the former metric looks artificially high.

Further assuaging shareholders should be the fact that PepsiCo commands excellent, investment-grade credit ratings: A1, Moody’s; A+ S&P. Profitability for the firm is outstanding.

Return on equity has averaged 51.8% over the last five years, while net margin has averaged 10.1%. ROE has been boosted by the balance sheet, but ROIC is often in a high-teens range.

Despite slowing growth, PepsiCo is still one of the world’s foremost branded food and beverage companies. And with economies of scale, a global distribution network, IP, R&D, brand power, and barriers to entry through established retail relationships with dedicated shelf space, the company does benefit from durable competitive advantages.

Of course, there are risks to consider. Regulation, litigation, and competition are omnipresent risks in every industry. Regulation, especially with the stance of the current HHS, could be an even larger hurdle for PepsiCo and its slate of processed foods.

Adding to the regulatory pressure, there are general and changing consumer trends toward healthier foods – a trend likely to be accelerated by GLP-1s – which may negatively impact sales across some of PepsiCo’s products that lean more into taste/enjoyment than outright health. GLP-1s may reduce overall demand for food consumption, generally, which would be a negative for PepsiCo.

Technology and the rise of alternative forms of media make it easier for new entrants to come into the market, advertise, make themselves known to consumers, and compete with entrenched giants like PepsiCo.

PepsiCo must continue to navigate different tastes in different markets, as well as evolving consumer tastes and preferences globally. The company’s international footprint exposes it to exchange rates and geopolitical risks (such as tariffs).

Input costs can be volatile. Passing on higher costs by raising prices on products can lead to lower volumes and strained relationships with retailers.

The balance sheet isn’t as strong as it used to be, which somewhat constrains PepsiCo’s broadening through acquisitions. Because of the law of large numbers, PepsiCo’s large size and thorough market saturation may be a serious headwind for future growth.

This isn’t a risk-free proposition, and no business is, but the stock’s 25% drop from recent all-time highs seems to compensate for plenty of risks...

Valuation

The stock is trading hands for a P/E ratio of 18.6, based on TTM Core EPS. This is a stock that has usually commanded an earnings multiple of well over 25.

Its P/CF ratio of 14.7 is further evidence of how undemanding the valuation has become, comparing favorably to its own five-year average of 17.8. And the yield, as noted earlier, is significantly higher than its own recent historical average.

So the stock looks cheap when looking at basic valuation metrics. But how cheap might it be? What would a rational estimate of intrinsic value look like?

I valued shares using a dividend discount model analysis. I factored in a 10% discount rate and a long-term dividend growth rate of 5.5%. With the payout ratio being as high as it is, along with the near-term expectation for fairly slow bottom-line growth, I don’t see room for dividend growth to come in much higher than this over the foreseeable future.

The company’s most recent dividend raise came in at 5%, and I think that’s a good baseline upon which to build realistic expectations from here. It’s actually quite possible that dividend growth over the next year or two is even lower than what I’m modeling in, but I’d anticipate a slight bounce from there once business normalizes and its higher-growth, healthier portfolio starts to shine. I’m leaning conservative here, but I do like to err on the side of caution.

The DDM analysis gives me a fair value of $133.40. The reason I use a dividend discount model analysis is because a business is ultimately equal to the sum of all the future cash flow it can provide. The DDM analysis is a tailored version of the discounted cash flow model analysis, as it simply substitutes dividends and dividend growth for cash flow and growth. It then discounts those future dividends back to the present day, to account for the time value of money since a dollar tomorrow is not worth the same amount as a dollar today.

I find it to be a fairly accurate way to value dividend growth stocks. My model shows a more appropriate level of valuation after the stock’s big drop. But we’ll now compare that valuation with where two professional stock analysis firms have come out at. This adds balance, depth, and perspective to our conclusion.

Morningstar, a leading and well-respected stock analysis firm, rates stocks on a 5-star system. 1 star would mean a stock is substantially overvalued; 5 stars would mean a stock is substantially undervalued. 3 stars would indicate roughly fair value. Morningstar rates PEP as a 4-star stock, with a fair value estimate of $166.00.

CFRA is another professional analysis firm, and I like to compare my valuation opinion to theirs to see if I’m out of line. They similarly rate stocks on a 1-5 star scale, with 1 star meaning a stock is a strong sell and 5 stars meaning a stock is a strong buy. 3 stars is a hold. CFRA rates PEP as a 4-star “buy”, with a 12-month target price of $170.00.

Perhaps my model was too cautious? Averaging the three numbers out gives us a final valuation of $156.47, which would indicate the stock is possibly 7% undervalued.

Bottom line: PepsiCo Inc. is an iconic snack food and beverage company with more than 125 years of successful operations. Although growth has slowed, this remains one of the premier businesses in its field. With a market-smashing yield, an acceptable payout ratio, high-single-digit dividend growth, more than 50 consecutive years of dividend increases, and the potential that shares are 7% undervalued, this blue-chip Dividend Aristocrat and Dividend King is ripe for long-term dividend growth investors to consider picking.

Note from D&I: How safe is PEP's dividend? We ran the stock through Simply Safe Dividends, and as we go to press, its Dividend Safety Score is 93. Dividend Safety Scores range from 0 to 100. A score of 50 is average, 75 or higher is excellent, and 25 or lower is weak. With this in mind, PEP's dividend appears Very Safe with an unlikely risk of being cut.

Disclosure: I’m long PEP.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: AIPI: If You Are Not AI Bear, Then This 38% Yielder Is For You
data.description:
Recently, I circulated an article on the REX FANG & Innovation Equity Premium Income ETF (FEPI) titled FEPI: This 25% Yielder Is About To Generate Even Bigger Income.

In the article, I laid out my justification for why FEPI could be viewed as a significant exception in the income-investing world. It is one of the rare securities, which offers a yield that is meaningfully above 20% and comes with no or very minor NAV erosion.

Usually, whenever a security produces anything that is significantly above 10%, the risks are most likely too high for prudent and sustainable income investors to step in.

However, after the publication of my FEPI piece, I received quite many comments and suggestions to consider the REX AI Equity Premium Income ETF (AIPI), which comes from the same REX Shares family but offers even greater yield than my high-conviction FEPI choice.

The yield on a TTM basis for AIPI stands at ~38%, while for FEPI it is slightly above 25%. So, we are talking about a ~13% monthly yield difference, which is definitely a material number. The delta of 13% itself could move the needle for the majority of income investors (and I'm not even considering the ~25% yielding base).

Now, the yield component is one thing, but for a full picture view, we have to always keep the price (and/or NAV) component with us in calculus. By doing so and comparing FEPI to AIPI, we will see that AIPI has still delivered superior results even though the massive yield would suggest that the opposite scenario should be more likely:

So far the initial story for AIPI looks quite promising.

Let me flesh out the underlying case in more detail.

Thesis

At its core, AIPI is indeed structurally similar to FEPI.

It is an efficiently structured (just 0.65% of the management fee) out-of-the-money covered call ETF, which takes direct equity stakes in certain securities, forming a rather concentrated basket of high volatility names against which options get sold.

And here already we have an important nuance to appreciate that AIPI does not write options against an aggregate or broad index like the Nasdaq-100 (QQQ) or the S&P 500 (SPY), which is what many covered call ETF household names such as the NEOS NASDAQ-100(R) High Income ETF (QQQI), the Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ), and the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) do.

The security-specific option overlay program is basically a double-edged sword.

On the one hand, it introduces a risk of specific security and their allocation weight-driven underperformance. For example, currently, the largest holding of AIPI is Palantir (PLTR), accounting for more than 10%, while for the QQQ, it is lower. If PLTR nose-dives, AIPI will likely underperform the index.

On the other hand, implementing this kind of approach enables the Fund to capture juicier option premiums due to a higher volatility that is associated with a more concentrated basket of stocks than holding a diversified pool of say, 100 or 500 securities (QQQ and SPY, respectively).

Speaking of AIPI's holdings, the top securities are the following:

And this is where the difference kicks in from FEPI. Namely, AIPI carries a more concentrated exposure towards the AI names, which have over the past couple of quarters and years delivered truly impressive results. They have been volatile, but mostly related to the upside swings.

Here is a relevant excerpt from the fund's prospectus, which describes how AIPI cherry-picks specific AI-biased securities:

Based on their degree of thematic purity, securities are admitted into the Eligible Universe and assigned to one of two categories:

(1) “Purity Leaders,” which are companies with a high relative revenue exposure to AI products, services, and activities;

and (2) “Key Enablers,” which are companies that play a crucial role in facilitating the business application of AI technology, even though their primary business activities may not be solely focused on AI products or services.

Securities in the Eligible Universe are ranked and selected as follow within each category:

A. Purity Leaders: Securities are ranked based on their trading volumes and the top 5 are selected for inclusion. Securities within this category are weighted by average daily value traded over the prior 12-months, subject to a 10% cap.

B. Key Enablers: Securities are ranked based on their market capitalization and the top 20 securities are selected for inclusion. Securities within this category are equally weighted.

In other words, AIPI is a pure-play bet on AI, just with a covered call overlay angle, which means that the upside is capped by the written option call strike values and the downside is largely open (i.e., the same as for the portfolio holdings), but with a slight nuance that the collected option premiums serve as a partial offset for the potential downside move.

Now, what is important to understand before I transition to the summary section and share my honest opinion on AIPI is how the ETF implements its covered call strategy in practice.

There are two fundamental aspects to this:

  1. As stated above, AIPI writes OTM call options on an individual security level. The magnitude of out-of-moneyness varies among the portfolio securities, but typically we are talking about 5% to 15%. For example, in NVIDIA Corporation's (NVDA) case, there is roughly 5% of upside participation open. For CrowdStrike Holdings, Inc. (CRWD) and Micron Technology (MU), it is 8% and 9%, respectively (but MU is already in the money because of the massive run-up that has happened in the past weeks/days). From this we can read that AIPI is not only about income but also about price appreciation, which in the long run comes in handy, serving as an offset for periods when market shocks take place.
  2. The duration of written call options is around 30 days, which gives a nice window of opportunity for stocks to recover in case there has been a steeper correction so that AIPI can avoid locking in losses and setting unfavorable upside caps that might limit its participation in the eventual recovery ride.

We can see how this substance has manifested itself in AIPI's total returns relative to the booming QQQ (AI world):

The presence of OTM covered calls in combination with a more pronounced AI tilt in AIPI's holding base has enabled yield investors to capture very similar returns to the pure-play index (just in an income-friendly fashion).

Granted, if we isolate the period to the past 6 months, we will see that AIPI has delivered ~7.5% in total returns, while the index is up by almost 11%. This divergence is primarily related to the security-selection issue, where AIPI's stronger emphasis on AI names has this time introduced a headwind. Namely, the non-top 10 QQQ index names have been lately delivering better returns than the AI giants.

So, the question is whether this trend will continue. The simple answer is we do not know, but given the rich valuations and the increased chatter in the market around the great rotation - i.e., capital flowing back from large caps into more reasonably valued small caps - we have to be ready that AIPI continues to lag behind the market.

The key is to avoid steep L-shaped sell-offs in the AI space so that AIPI can skip locking in losses after the 30-day OTM option window expires. In my view, considering the past couple of years, where each dip has been followed by a recovery and the underlying case for the AI solutions, I would feel comfortable allocating AIPI in my portfolio as a tactical yield diversifier and enhancer.

The Bottom Line

Honestly speaking, I was quite skeptical about AIPI before I opened the financial documents and started to assess the case deeper.

I see one important and potentially drastic risk in AIPI, which is the massive sell-off of AI names. If that happens and there is a sudden risk-off mode assumed against the richly priced AI stocks, AIPI will get hammered, just as its income. For example, when the tariff tantrum occurred, AIPI dropped from ~$50 per share to $38 per share, and its monthly income distribution amount decreased from $1.45 per share to $1.10 per share.

The good thing here was that AIPI follows an OTM and 30-day expiration strategy, which allowed the vehicle to participate in the recovery and gradually increase the distributions from that "tariff-related plunge" moment. Yet, the rhetorical question is what would happen if the sell-off was not followed by a V-shape recovery, but instead there was an L-shape process in place. Nothing good.

Having said that, we have to recognize that this kind of argument can be applied to FEPI, JEPQ, QQQI, and basically almost any other tech-related covered call index fund (and also for the pure-play indices themselves). The difference in AIPI's case is that under such a pessimistic L-shaped bear market, the impact will probably be much stronger due to the more focused AI strategy.

So, the way I would treat AIPI is like a minor, tactical income enhancer that complements more diversified FEPI and, especially, QQQI exposures. And these AI-related covered call exposures themselves should be kept limited and balanced with covered call vehicles, which harvest vol income from more durable underlying holdings like midstream partnerships (MLPI) and gold (IAUI).

Last but not least, I want to make it clear that AIPI distributes income, which comes in the form of return-of-capital (due to the option premiums being viewed as principal capital) that enables a more tax-efficient treatment.

data.title: LCI Industries: Massive Upside Is Possible
data.description:
The last few months have been exceptionally positive for shareholders of LCI Industries (LCII). Since I reaffirmed the company as a ‘buy’ candidate back in September of last year, the stock has shot up 31.1%. That is drastically higher than the 5.5% increase that the S&P 500 experienced. However, to be fair, since I originally rated the firm a ‘buy’ in October of the previous year, the stock is up a more modest 22.1%. That is above the 19.4% increase that the market enjoyed. But it's not drastically higher.

This overall move can be chalked up to growing revenue, profits, and cash flows. This is interesting considering the challenges that the industry has faced. RV shipments have struggled for years now. But with management now forecasting a more robust recovery than before, and the expectation of additional organic growth over the next couple of years, I am certainly optimistic. I would argue, in fact, that this is a very solid prospect, even if industry conditions deteriorate and negatively affect the company. This is why I have no problem rating it a ‘buy’ at this time.

The picture just keeps getting better

Unfortunately, at this moment, investors don't have access to data covering the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. But we do have data covering through the third quarter. During that time, revenue for the company came in strong at $1.04 billion. This happened to be 13.2% above the $915.5 million the company reported the previous year. Interestingly, this came in spite of the fact that there were certain challenges that the company demonstrated. While for the trailing 12 months ending in September of 2025, the number of wholesale RV units sold came in 4.8% above what the industry had seen the previous year, while the number of retail RV units sold grew by only 0.5%. For the most recent quarter on its own, the industry exhibited weakness. For instance, wholesale unit shipments were down 4.1% year over year, and the retail increase was a paltry 1.3%.

The last few years now have been really challenging for the RV market. The COVID-19 pandemic caused a surge in demand for social distancing. When you add on top of this hefty government stimulus, the end result was a flurry of buying activity in the RV market. In 2020, for instance, RV shipments amounted to only 430,412 units. But the next year, in 2021, they were 600,240 units. This essentially front-loaded significant demand. And by 2023, unit shipments had bottomed out at 313,174 units.

We have started to see a recovery in this market. In 2024, shipments amounted to 333,733 units. Management also increased their guidance for 2025. Right now, they estimate between 340,000 and 350,000 unit sales for this year. That compares to the 337,000 previously estimated at the midpoint. The company now believes that 2026 will see unit shipments of between 345,000 and 360,000 units. The midpoint there translates to 352,500 units. But in their previous forecasts, unit shipments amounted to 349,000.

Short term, I actually think we could see some weakness. After all, as I have detailed in prior articles, I firmly believe the US is heading toward a recession. Consumer confidence levels are near all-time lows, and we can expect that to weigh on buying activity in a market that is purely discretionary and that requires large capital investments. The good news is that, after economic conditions bottom out, we should see some nice recovery here. And that's because interest rates are likely to drop faster than what others anticipate. That should grease the wheels on purchases at some point. So while I think that their forecast for 2026 may be aggressive, I don't think the future for the company or the industry looks bad.

As revenue for the company improved, profitability skyrocketed. Net income jumped from $35.6 million all the way to $62.5 million. It is important to keep in mind, however, that a lot of this improvement came from a $19.7 million gain on the sale of real estate. Actual adjusted net profits grew a more modest, but still very impressive, 35.1% from $35.6 million to $48.1 million. Operating cash flow jumped from $78.4 million to $97.2 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get growth from $74.2 million to $83.3 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business increased from $85.2 million to $105.9 million.

Management has a long history of doing well in this space. Some of this can be attributed to new products and market share gains. For instance, management said that in 2025, it would capture annualized year over year organic content growth of between 3% and 5%. This is basically the amount of content that goes into each RV. And for 2026, it expects the same. And all of this should be driven solely by innovation opportunities.

This kind of innovation, and the corresponding ability of the company to capture market share from other competitors, has had a huge positive impact on operations. In fact, from the year 2000 to the year 2024, revenue jumped from $288 million to $3.7 billion.

This is a monumental amount of upside, driven by innovation, investments in organic growth opportunities, and the like. The company has made various investments in innovative opportunities, such as anti-lock brakes, certain types of windows, chill lube, and the like. In fact, for the innovations that it discussed in its investor presentation for October of last year, the total addressable markets opened up to it solely by these types of investments amounted to $470 million.

In the chart above, you can also see financial results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time of the previous year. Revenue, profits, and cash flows, have all increased. This is positive, especially considering how sluggish the industry has been as of late. What's really exciting, however, is that management expects additional growth opportunities. The firm is targeting $5 billion in revenue by 2027. That compares to the $3.7 billion the business reported for 2024. With that, we are likely to see additional bottom line growth as well. For last year, I anticipate adjusted net profits of $175.5 million. Adjusted operating cash flow would be $319.2 million, while EBITDA would amount to $390 million. But if the firm achieves its targets, then we would be looking at readings of $327.4 million, $658.2 million, and $787.9 million, respectively.

Using these estimates, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. For the 2025 figures, the stock is attractive. But just barely. But if we project things out for 2027 based on the guidance management has provided, shares start to look outright cheap. In the chart below, I also compared the company to five other similar firms. On a price to operating cash flow basis, it's not all that special relative to other comparable firms. But when it comes to the price-to-earnings approach, it is the cheapest of the group. Meanwhile, when it comes to the EV to EBITDA approach, only one of the companies ended up being cheaper than it is.

In the event that management can achieve their target for 2027, it is likely that upside could be quite large from where it is today. If the firm continues to trade at the same multiples it's currently trading at, we would be looking at an annualized upside of 36.5%. That's based on the current price-to-earnings multiple compared to what I calculated for 2027. On a price-to-adjusted-operating-cash-flow basis, upside would be 43.7% annually. And when it comes to the EV to EBITDA approach, we would be looking at a 50.6% upside per annum. This is significant. Even if the company can achieve half of this annualized upside, we would be looking at something really impressive.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I must say that I remain bullish on LCI Industries. Would I be surprised if short-term economic conditions make financial performance for the business worse? No, I wouldn't be. But when we look at the picture as a whole, I see an attractively priced firm that should continue to achieve nice appreciation. This makes it a clear and obvious ‘buy’ as far as I am concerned.

data.title: Celestica: I Was Wrong - Buy Ahead Of Q4 Earnings
data.description:
I Underestimated Celestica's Operating Leverage Initially

Celestica Inc. (CLS) stock has outrun my October prediction of a highly likely pullback, gaining over 10% since then (the S&P 500 gave us only 3-4% for the same period). I had a "Hold" rating on CLS because I thought the price action had already reflected all the upcoming margin expansion and the business growth from the firm's hyperscale-focused CCS segment. I still don't see excessive margins from CCS ahead, but clearly I was wrong in how I assessed Celestica's operating leverage amid the ongoing shift toward the HPS portfolio. The stock price looks very expensive at first glance, but I think CLS might grow into its current multiples as the structural shift from EMS toward ODM dominance continues and the catalysts for the next few years unfold. That's why I'm upgrading CLS from "Hold" to "Buy".

When Low Margins Don't Matter

Celestica reported for its Q3 2025 numbers a week after my initiation, and it showed $3.19 billion in sales (+27.6% YoY) and $1.58 in adjusted EPS (+51.9% YoY), beating the consensus estimates by 5.1% and 6.03%, respectively, according to Seeking Alpha. Here we can draw a parallel to the growth we've seen in 2024, when CLS achieved a 21% YoY growth in sales amid 58% YoY growth in EPS as the firm's operating margin expanded from 5.5% to 6.5% at the time. As of Q3 2025, the adjusted EBIT margin reached 7.6%, so the fiscal 2025 basically repeated the success of the fiscal 2024 in that regard (and the price action followed a similar path). The company's business model setup doesn't seem to require a tremendous margin expansion to reach a massive operating leverage.

The question now is, "Will Celestica's margin keep climbing in 2026?"

The management said on the call that yes, they're guiding for 7.8% by the end of the fiscal 2026, with an excess of EPS over revenue growth at ~8%:

The outlook for the financial performance of the business in 2026 continues to be very strong. We anticipate revenue of $16.0 billion, representing 31% growth compared to our 2025 outlook. At the segment level, CCS revenue is expected to grow by approximately 40%, driven by strong market tailwinds and program ramps in both our enterprise and communications end markets.

We expect non-GAAP operating margin to expand by 40 basis points to 7.8%, driven by a favorable mix and productivity improvements. Our non-GAAP adjusted EPS is projected to be $8.20, which would represent a 39% increase year-over-year.

Source: Celestica's earnings call for Q3 2025.

So, even a small 0.2% rise in non-GAAP EBIT margin in 2027 should keep the firm's solid operating leverage in place and lead to a superior EPS growth rate in 2026. Personally, I see no signs of slowing and limited risks to margins as long as the medium-term bullish catalysts start to play out and potentially contribute to above-consensus growth rates.

Clear Bullish Catalysts Ahead

We're still in the middle of the Gen AI competitive race, where all LLM providers try to increase the number of parameters for better models' outputs and hence better usability. Thus, the networking market is moving from 800G to 1.6 Terabit (1.6T), requiring a new type of switching. I believe it's the primary bullish catalyst for Celestica's growth ahead as the company has already introduced the DS6000 and DS6001 switch families (they're on the Broadcom Tomahawk 6 chipset), doubling the capacity of existing 800G solutions.

The DS6000 series reduces the number of required switches by 80%, according to another press release, thus "drastically cutting latency and power consumption", which is exactly what hyperscalers will be willing to pay for over the next few years, in my opinion. Also, their DS6001 switch was designed for hybrid air/liquid cooling environments, specifically for AI data centers that run training models consuming megawatts of power, where traditional air cooling is no longer sufficient. I think that as CLS integrates these offerings into its rack-scale solutions, they'll achieve the system level that few competitors can replicate.

Celestica is going to ramp the production of 1.6T programs in 2026 and 2027, thus seeing a very favorable setup for its business beyond just 2026.

Our view for 2027 assumes multiple ramps with hyperscaler customers with new programs supporting the 1.6T upgrade cycle, including scale-up solutions and a next-generation custom ASIC compute platform. We also anticipate the commencement of mass production of our rack-scale custom AI system program with the digital native customer. As a result, we expect these strong growth dynamics to persist. And in support of this, we are aligning our capacity with our customers, assuming that this trajectory continues into 2027.

Source: Celestica's earnings call for Q3 2025

Another bullish catalyst supporting the continuation of the current upcycle is the rise of the custom ASIC compute market, where Celestica has great expertise, as it has been co-developing ASIC-based servers optimized for specific AI workloads.

According to ResearchAndMarkets' study, the custom ASIC market is projected to climb from $27 billion in 2024 to over $43 billion by 2030 (CAGR = 8.23%), so for Celestica, this niche is going to become a great growth addition, in my understanding (likely adding to margin expansion as well).

Another catalyst that adds to Celestica's growth story beyond just 2026 is the planned manufacturing footprint expansion in Texas, where the management expects an increase in power consumption by 10x by 2027. Plus, they're growing in Monterrey, Mexico, and it provides shorter lead times and can start to contribute to the overall growth rates already this year, in my view.

When we combine all the above-discussed catalysts, I think it's becoming clear that Celestica doesn't need to increase its EBIT margin by another 100 bps this year (and next year) to see its EPS grow by 30-40%. The bottom line expansion might come organically and naturally from the top line expansion, and that's where Celestica stands out with its consistency of growth:

When Valuation Peaks Don't Matter

Right now, Celestica is trading above a 50x FY2025 price-to-earnings ratio, which is the peak for the stock when we look back to 10-20 years ago:

But the calendar 2025 is gone, and when Celestica reports for Q4 (in less than 2 weeks), we should start to see FY2026 as its default P/E ratio. When it comes to FY2026, CLS is valued at only 37x. While it still looks expensive to some value investors, I guess, it's not that excessive for the level of EPS growth we're getting. The effective PEG will be below 1x. For FY2027 earnings, which are becoming "1-year forward" now, the stock's PEG drops to ~0.65x, and that's how CLS starts to look underestimated, of the current consensus is spot on.

I dare to conclude here that Celestica's seeming valuation peaks don't really matter. CLS isn't as cheap as it used to be just a few months ago. The rally has given investors the "easy money" already. But if the catalysts that I discussed earlier materialize—at least to some extent—CLS is likely to enjoy another round of positive earnings revisions that should make the forward multiples a steal if the stock price doesn't adjust higher.

I'd put a 10-15% premium on top of today's FY2027 EPS consensus and assume a P/E multiple of 25-30x. The resulting price target for the next 12-16 months arrives at $365.37, and it's 16.5% above the current stock price.

Previewing Celestica's Q4 Earnings

Celestica's management guided for $3.45 billion in revenues at the midpoint, implying a YoY expansion of 36%. The EPS should increase by 56% (to $1.73 at the midpoint), so again, the operating leverage should look good. The market prices premiums on both the top and bottom lines because Celestica usually beats its midpoints.

The history shows that in addition to the midpoints, CLS usually beats the tops of their guidance ranges. And the above consensus is set below the tops ($3.49 billion vs. $3.575 billion and $1.76 vs. $1.81). The odds for beating look very high to me, especially when we assume that the 1.6T transition is already going. My core expectations from Celestica's Q4:

  1. The company beats on both revenue and EPS.
  2. The management raises the previous FY2026 guidance (or at least holds it).
  3. The commentary for 2026 and 2027 sounds bullish.

If all these events happen, CLS's price should naturally be positive.

Risks To My Views

One of the biggest risks to my thesis is that CLS gets ~59% of its total revenues from just 3 key customers, who might decide to switch to another peer over time. The current rally in CLS is driven by the firm's fundamentals, which presumably stem from these three customers' CAPEX plans. So, if they decide to slow down their spending for any reason, Celestica's backlog will decline, and the market will no longer expect the 40%+ growth in its FY2027 EPS. Thus, CLS won't have any justifications for trading as high as it's trading now. Also, we can add geopolitical risks and supply chain disruptions to the list, as Celestica's main operations are conducted outside of the U.S. (they're mainly producing in Asia).

Conclusion

I admit that I was wrong in my previous forecast, where I cited a slowdown in Celestica's margin expansion as the reason for expecting a correction. Now, I see that the level of operating leverage is insanely strong, and CLS doesn't need to grow its EBIT margin by 100 bps yearly to deliver exceptional EPS expansion. What it needs to do is deliver on its plans and ensure the existing catalysts materialize at least partially. Based on the level of execution we've seen in the past, I think that Celestica can do it. I see an undervaluation of 16.5% to the current price, upgrading CLS to "Buy".
